ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani). FOODON_03311069 (chicken curry) is the closest parent class. A more specific term for butter chicken in creamy tomato sauce does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Butter Chicken curry. A0CKM (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest parent. The dish includes a creamy tomato-butter sauce which is not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Butter Chicken or Murgh Makhani as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT food concepts do not extend to this level of Indian cuisine specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Butter Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising boneless chicken pieces in a generous portion of creamy tomato-butter gravy, usually served alongside rice or naan bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, with gravy — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for chicken curry with gravy. Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) is a richer variant with additional butter and cream. Values for energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are adapted upward to reflect the higher fat content from butter and cream, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken, broiler or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, roasted' (FDC ID 171477) and published recipe-level analyses. The adaptation is documented and confidence is set to medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-sauce curry dishes. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are tomato, onion, and cream, all of which are low-GI ingredients. The protein and fat content of the dish further attenuates glycemic response. Analogous reference: mixed meat curry dishes with low-GI sauce components, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 5.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 5.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving of Butter Chicken contributes approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~5.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter Chicken has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.8 per standard serving, making it a relatively safe choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; pairing with cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice is preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cream (~4.2 g saturated fat per 100 g; ~12.6 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Butter Chicken provides approximately 12.6 g of saturated fat, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g, request preparation with reduced butter and cream, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken, known in Hindi as Murgh Makhani or Murg Makhani, is one of the most internationally recognised North Indian curry dishes. It was reportedly created in the 1950s at Moti Mahal restaurant in Delhi by Kundan Lal Gujral and Kundan Lal Jaggi, who combined leftover tandoori chicken with a rich tomato-butter-cream sauce. The dish consists of boneless chicken pieces — typically marinated overnight in yoghurt and spices including garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli — that are first roasted in a tandoor oven to develop a smoky char, then simmered in a velvety sauce made from pureed tomatoes, butter, cream, onion, garlic, ginger, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The result is a mildly spiced, deeply aromatic curry with a characteristic orange-red colour, a silky smooth texture, and a balanced flavour profile that is simultaneously tangy from the tomatoes, rich from the butter and cream, and subtly smoky from the tandoor cooking. Nutritionally, Butter Chicken provides approximately 150 kcal per 100 g, with 12.5 g protein, 9 g fat (of which 4.2 g is saturated), and 5.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of protein and B vitamins from the chicken, and provides moderate amounts of vitamin A and iron from the tomato-spice base. The sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 380 mg per 100 g, and the saturated fat from butter and cream warrants attention for cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for glycemic management when served without high-GI accompaniments. In Singapore, Butter Chicken is widely available at North Indian restaurants, Indian hawker stalls, and food delivery platforms, and is commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter chicken?
Is butter chicken good for diabetics?
Is butter chicken high in sodium?
Can I eat butter chicken with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Butter Chicken entry was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry with gravy data, adjusted upward for the higher butter and cream content characteristic of Murgh Makhani, and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry with gravy (2017 revised edition) with upward adjustment for butter and cream content; no exact Butter Chicken entry exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the low-GI ingredient profile (tomato, cream, onion) and the attenuating effect of protein and fat; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Makhani was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. 5. Halal status is marked true as the dish is prepared with chicken and dairy; however, halal certification depends on the specific restaurant and sourcing. 6. The input record listed two field survey restaurants (Bawarchi North Indian Cuisine and Bombay Grill); these are preserved as context but do not constitute nutritional evidence. 7. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a typical Singapore restaurant portion of butter chicken curry; actual restaurant portions may vary from 250 g to 400 g. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and B-vitamin values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken breast and tomato-based sauce components as ICMR-NIN does not report all micronutrients for this dish.
